subject: Careers
topic: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 3: Biases That Shape How We Judge People

worksheet.title: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 3: Biases That Shape How We Judge People
anchor: Learning goal: I can identify fundamental attribution error, self-serving bias, and in-group bias, and I can explain why social judgments are often incomplete.
Use this worksheet during the mini-lesson, then move into work time on your career presentation.

questions.q_text: Quick start: Why is it risky to judge a person or job too quickly?
Key notes: Fundamental attribution error means explaining a person's behaviour by their personality while ignoring the situation around them. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: Self-serving bias means taking credit for success but blaming outside causes for mistakes or weak results. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: In-group bias means preferring or trusting people and groups that feel most like 'us.' Write your own example or note.

Practice: Someone judges a worker's character without considering stress, workload, or staffing. Bias: ____________________
A student says success happened because they are talented, but weak results happened because of everything else. Bias: ____________________
A career gets dismissed because a friend group does not value it. Bias: ____________________
Apply it to your career presentation: Write one example where situation matters more than personality.
Apply it to your career presentation: What is one honest strength and one honest area to improve in your own presentation work?
Apply it to your career presentation: How might friend-group opinion affect career choices in school?
Exit reflection: How can social bias affect career choices? Write 2–3 clear sentences.

self_check: I can name fundamental attribution error, self-serving bias, and in-group bias.
I connected social judgment bias to career choices or presentation work.
I am ready to continue revising or building my presentation.
